grammarLessons.name: Frequency Adverbs — siempre, a veces, nunca
Object Pronouns — lo, la, me, te

cards.grammarNote:
Use **ser** for professions — and omit the article:
**Soy médico** ✓ (not *Soy un médico*) · **Es enfermera** · **Es ingeniero**
The article returns only when the noun is modified: **Soy un buen médico**

Gender forms: **el enfermero** (male nurse) / **la enfermera** (female nurse).
From Latin **infirmus** (sick/weak) — compare English "infirmary".
Use **ser** for the profession without an article: **Es enfermera** (She is a nurse).

Gender forms: **el ingeniero** / **la ingeniera**.
From Latin **ingenium** (cleverness, talent, natural ability) — the same root as English "engine" and "ingenious".
Use **ser** without an article: **Mi hermano es ingeniero** (My brother is an engineer).

Gender forms: **el programador** / **la programadora**.
**Trabajo como programador** uses **como** (as/like) — an alternative to **ser**: **soy programador** and **trabajo como programador** both work.
**Trabajar como** is especially natural when describing a current role rather than a permanent identity.

Gender forms: **el cocinero** / **la cocinera**.
Note the double meaning of **cocina**: it means both "kitchen" (the room) and "cooking/cuisine" (the activity).
**La cocina italiana** = Italian cuisine · **trabajar en la cocina** = to work in the kitchen.
A professional chef is often called **el/la chef** (borrowed from French).
cards.englishPhrase: The cook works in the kitchen.
cards.phrase: El cocinero trabaja en la cocina.

cards.grammarNote:
Gender forms: **el camarero** / **la camarera** (Spain).
In Latin America you'll often hear **el mesero / la mesera** instead.
**Trajo** is the preterite (past tense) of **traer** (to bring) — an irregular verb: traje, trajiste, trajo, trajimos, trajeron.
Note the accent on **menú** — required because the stress falls on the final syllable.

**Gerente** is a shared-gender noun: **el gerente** / **la gerente** (the ending doesn't change).
This is common with nouns ending in **-ente** or **-ante**: **el/la estudiante**, **el/la presidente**, **el/la asistente**.
Another word for manager: **el/la director(a)** (director), **el/la jefe/jefa** (boss/chief).

**Estudiante** is a shared-gender noun: **el estudiante** / **la estudiante** — the word itself doesn't change.
This is typical of nouns ending in **-nte**: **el/la asistente**, **el/la presidente**, **el/la gerente**.
Use **ser** without an article for professions/roles: **Soy estudiante** (I am a student) — not *Soy un estudiante*.

Gender forms: **el arquitecto** / **la arquitecta**.
From Greek **arkhitekton** (master builder): **arkhi-** (chief) + **tekton** (builder) — same root as English "architect".
Use **ser** without an article: **Él es arquitecto** (He is an architect).
Related word: **la arquitectura** (architecture).

Gender forms: **el abogado** / **la abogada**.
From Latin **advocatus** (one called to help) — same root as English "advocate".
Notice the example: **Mi hermana es abogada** — the profession agrees in gender with the subject (**hermana** → **abogada**).
Related: **el derecho** (law/right), **el bufete** (law firm).

**Jugar a** + sport is the standard construction: **jugar al fútbol** (al = a + el).
Stem-changing verb: **jugar** (u→ue): **juego, juegas, juega, jugamos, jugáis, juegan**.
Don't confuse with **tocar** — use **jugar** for games/sports, **tocar** for instruments.
**Fútbol** is borrowed from English "football" — note the accent to mark Spanish stress rules.
cards.englishPhrase: I love playing soccer with my friends.
cards.phrase: Me encanta jugar al fútbol con mis amigos.

cards.grammarNote:
Gender forms: **jubilado** (m) / **jubilada** (f).
Crucially, this uses **estar** not **ser**: **Mi padre está jubilado** — retirement is a state, not an inherent identity.
From **júbilo** (joy/jubilation) — retirement was historically associated with celebration.
The verb is **jubilarse** (reflexive): **Se jubiló el año pasado** (She retired last year).

**Me gusta leer** — with **gustar**, use the infinitive for activities: **Me gusta leer, nadar, cocinar**.
For multiple activities, **gustan** is NOT used — keep **gusta** with a list of infinitives: **Me gusta leer y cocinar**.
**Leer** has a spelling change in the gerund: **leyendo** (not *leiendo*) — the unstressed **i** between vowels becomes **y**.
Same pattern: **creer → creyendo**, **caer → cayendo**.

**A ella le gusta escribir** — the **a + pronoun** construction clarifies who likes something, since **le** alone is ambiguous (could be he/she/you formal).
**Escribir** vs. **escribir a mano** (to write by hand) vs. **teclear** (to type).
Related nouns: **la escritura** (writing/script), **el escritor / la escritora** (writer), **el cuento** (short story).
cards.phrase: A ella le gusta escribir cuentos.

cards.grammarNote:
**A él le encanta pintar** — **encantar** is a stronger form of **gustar** (to love, to adore).
Both **gustar** and **encantar** are "reverse" verbs: the thing enjoyed is the grammatical subject.
**Pintar** (to paint) → **el pintor / la pintora** (painter), **la pintura** (painting/paint), **el cuadro** (painting/picture).
Note: **pintar** covers both painting a wall and creating art — context distinguishes them.

**Dibujar** = to draw (art/sketching). Not to be confused with **dibujos animados** (cartoons/animated drawings).
**Me gusta dibujar** — infinitive after **gustar** for activities.
Related nouns: **el dibujo** (drawing/sketch), **el dibujante / la dibujante** (illustrator/draughtsperson).
Compare: **pintar** (to paint with color), **dibujar** (to draw with lines/pencil).

**La fotografía** — the definite article **la** is used when talking about a subject/field in the abstract: **La fotografía es mi pasatiempo** (Photography is my hobby).
Related words: **la foto** (photo, informal shortening), **el fotógrafo / la fotógrafa** (photographer), **sacar/tomar una foto** (to take a photo).
**Pasatiempo** (pastime) = **pasar** (to pass) + **tiempo** (time) — a transparent compound.

**Escuchar música** = to listen to music — **escuchar** already means "to listen to", so no preposition is needed (unlike English).
Don't confuse **escuchar** (to listen to, attentively) with **oír** (to hear, passively): **Oigo música** (I hear music in the background) vs. **Escucho música** (I'm actively listening to music).
**Todos los días** = every day — note **todos** (all, masculine plural) agrees with **días**.
cards.englishPhrase: I listen to music every day.

cards.grammarNote:
**Cantar** = to sing. The person who sings is **el cantante / la cantante** (singer) — a shared-gender noun ending in **-ante**.
**A ella le encanta cantar** — **encantar** (to love/adore) works just like **gustar** but is stronger.
Related: **la canción** (song), **el coro** (choir/chorus), **afinar** (to tune/to sing in tune).

**Tocar** is the correct verb for playing an instrument — never **jugar**.
**Toco la guitarra** · **toca el piano** · **tocamos el violín**
Use **jugar** for sports and games: **jugar al fútbol**, **jugar ajedrez**.
Instruments take the definite article: **toco la guitarra** (not *toco guitarra*).
Related: **el instrumento** (instrument), **la guitarra acústica / eléctrica** (acoustic / electric guitar).

**Nos encanta bailar** — **nos** is the indirect object pronoun for "us".
**Encantar** conjugates to agree with the grammatical subject (what is loved), not the person: **nos encanta** (one thing) vs. **nos encantan** (multiple things).
Related: **el baile** (dance/dancing), **la bailarina / el bailarín** (ballet dancer), **el salón de baile** (ballroom).
**Bailar** is also used in expressions: **bailar salsa/tango/flamenco**.

**Cocinar** (to cook) vs. **cocina** (kitchen / cuisine) — same root, different form.
**Me gusta cocinar** — infinitive after **gustar** for activities.
For professional cooking, use **el cocinero / la cocinera** (cook) or **el/la chef**.
**Practicar** is used for hobbies/sports in general, but **cocinar** is the direct verb: say **me gusta cocinar**, not *practico la cocina* (unnatural).

**La jardinería** — abstract/field nouns take the definite article: **La jardinería es relajante**.
From **el jardín** (garden) + **-ería** (a suffix indicating a craft, trade, or place): **la panadería** (bakery), **la carpintería** (carpentry).
Related: **el jardinero / la jardinera** (gardener), **plantar** (to plant), **regar** (to water plants — stem-changing: **riego, riegas...**).

**Hacer senderismo** = to go hiking — many outdoor activities in Spanish use **hacer**: **hacer senderismo**, **hacer yoga**, **hacer ejercicio**, **hacer surf**.
Alternatively: **ir de senderismo** (to go hiking).
**Senderismo** comes from **sendero** (path/trail).
Related: **la montaña** (mountain), **el monte** (hill/mountain/wilderness), **la ruta** (route/trail).
cards.englishPhrase: I like hiking in the mountains.
cards.phrase: Me gusta hacer senderismo en la montaña.

cards.grammarNote:
**Correr** = to run (a regular -er verb).
**Corro** (I run), **corres**, **corre**, **corremos**, **corréis**, **corren**.
**Todas las mañanas** = every morning — **todas** (all, feminine plural) agrees with **mañanas**.
Compare: **correr** (to run in general) vs. **trotar** (to jog at a light pace) vs. **hacer running** (to go for a run, borrowed term).

**Trotar** = to jog/trot (a regular -ar verb).
**Trota** = he/she jogs — the third-person singular present ending for -ar verbs.
Note: **trotar** is more common in Latin America; in Spain you might hear **hacer footing** or **correr** for jogging.
**En el parque** — **en** is used for location (in/at): **en casa**, **en el trabajo**, **en el parque**.

**Andar en bicicleta** = to ride a bike — literally "to walk/go by bicycle".
Also common: **montar en bicicleta** (especially in Spain) or **ir en bici** (informal).
**Andar** here means "to go/travel" rather than "to walk on foot".
**Los fines de semana** = on weekends — **el fin de semana** (the weekend), plural **los fines de semana**.
cards.phrase: Ando en bicicleta los fines de semana.

cards.grammarNote:
**Nadar** = to swim (a regular -ar verb).
**Me gusta nadar** — infinitive after **gustar** for activities.
For "to go swimming" use **ir a nadar** or **ir a la piscina** (to go to the pool).
Related: **la piscina** (pool), **el mar** (sea), **la playa** (beach), **el nadador / la nadadora** (swimmer).
**Practicar la natación** (to practice swimming) is also used in more formal contexts.

**Hacer yoga** = to do yoga — use **hacer** for exercise/wellness activities: **hacer yoga**, **hacer ejercicio**, **hacer pilates**.
**Yoga** is a masculine noun borrowed from Sanskrit: **el yoga** (though the article is often omitted in practice).
**Todas las mañanas** = every morning — **todas** (feminine plural) agrees with **mañanas**.
Frequency expressions: **todos los días** (every day), **todas las semanas** (every week), **cada mañana** (each morning).
cards.phrase: Ella hace yoga todas las mañanas.

cards.grammarNote:
**Hacer ejercicio** = to exercise — **hacer** is the go-to verb for physical activities.
**Voy al gimnasio** — **al** is the mandatory contraction of **a + el**: never *a el gimnasio*.
**Para + infinitive** expresses purpose: **voy al gimnasio para hacer ejercicio** (I go to the gym in order to exercise).
Related: **el gimnasio** (gym), **entrenarse** (to train/work out), **el entrenador / la entrenadora** (trainer).
cards.englishPhrase: I go to the gym for exercise.
cards.phrase: Voy al gimnasio para hacer ejercicio.

cards.grammarNote:
**Me encanta viajar** — **encantar** + infinitive = to love doing something.
**Viajar** is a regular -ar verb: **viajo, viajas, viaja, viajamos, viajáis, viajan**.
Related: **el viaje** (trip/journey), **el viajero / la viajera** (traveler), **viajar al extranjero** (to travel abroad).
**Ir de viaje** (to go on a trip) is a common alternative expression.

**Acampar** = to camp (a regular -ar verb). Also: **ir de camping** (borrowed term, common in Spain).
**Acampamos** = we camp/we are camping — first-person plural present.
Note: **en verano** (in summer) — seasons use **en** without an article: **en verano**, **en invierno**, **en primavera**, **en otoño**.
Related: **la tienda de campaña** (tent), **la hoguera** (campfire), **el saco de dormir** (sleeping bag).
cards.englishPhrase: We go camping in the summer.

cards.grammarNote:
Stem-changing verb: **jugar** (u→ue): **juego, juegas, juega, jugamos, jugáis, juegan**.
With video games, **jugar** is used directly or with **a**: **juega videojuegos** or **juega a los videojuegos**.
Compare with sports: **jugar al fútbol** (requires **al**) — with videojuegos, the **a** is often dropped in informal speech.
**Por la noche** = at night/in the evening — compare: **por la mañana** (in the morning), **por la tarde** (in the afternoon).
cards.englishPhrase: He plays video games at night.
cards.phrase: Él juega videojuegos por la noche.

cards.grammarNote:
**Ver** = to watch/see (an irregular yo form: **veo**): **veo, ves, ve, vemos, veis, ven**.
**Me gusta ver películas** — infinitive after **gustar**.
Distinction: **ver la tele/televisión** (to watch TV), **ver una película** (to watch a movie), **ver una serie** (to watch a series/show).
**Películas** (movies) from **película** — originally "film strip" (related to **piel**, skin/film).

**Escuchar** = to listen to — the "to" is built in, no extra preposition needed.
**A ella le gusta escuchar música** — the **a ella** clarifies who likes it, because **le** alone is ambiguous.
Distinction: **escuchar** (active, attentive listening) vs. **oír** (passive hearing).
Related: **el oyente** (listener), **la canción** (song), **el álbum** (album).
cards.phrase: A ella le gusta escuchar música.

cards.grammarNote:
**Artesanías** (crafts) — from **artesano / artesana** (craftsperson/artisan), which comes from Latin **artisanus**.
Also used: **manualidades** (handicrafts, DIY crafts — literally "hand activities", from **mano**).
**Están haciendo** = they are making — present progressive: **estar** + gerund.
For ongoing actions in Spanish, the simple present is often preferred over the progressive for habits: **hacen artesanías los sábados** (they make crafts on Saturdays).

**Ajedrez** comes from Arabic **al-shatranj**, itself from Sanskrit **chaturanga** (four divisions of an army).
With chess, **jugar** is used: **¿Juegas ajedrez?** or **¿Juegas al ajedrez?** — both are correct.
Stem-changing verb: **jugar** (u→ue): **juego, juegas, juega, jugamos, jugáis, juegan**.
Related: **el tablero** (board), **la pieza** (piece), **el rey** (king), **la reina** (queen), **jaque mate** (checkmate).

**Juegos de mesa** = board games — literally "table games".
Stem-changing verb: **jugar** (u→ue): **jugamos** (we play — note: no stem change in nosotros/vosotros forms).
**Los fines de semana** = on weekends — the definite article **los** is used for recurring time: **los lunes** (on Mondays), **los sábados** (on Saturdays).
Related: **el juego** (game), **jugar a las cartas** (to play cards), **el dado** (die/dice).
cards.englishPhrase: We play board games on weekends.
cards.phrase: Jugamos juegos de mesa los fines de semana.

cards.grammarNote:
**Trabajar** is a regular -ar verb: **trabajo, trabajas, trabaja, trabajamos, trabajáis, trabajan**.
Preposition choices: **trabajar en** + place (**trabajo en un hospital** — I work in a hospital), **trabajar como** + profession (**trabajo como enfermera** — I work as a nurse), **trabajar de** + profession (also common: **trabajo de cocinero**).
**Dedicarse a** = to devote oneself to / to work as: **Se dedica a la medicina** (She devotes herself to medicine).

**Ganar** = to earn / to win / to gain (a regular -ar verb).
Context determines meaning: **ganar dinero** (to earn money), **ganar un partido** (to win a game), **ganar experiencia** (to gain experience).
**Buen** vs. **bueno**: the adjective **bueno** shortens to **buen** before a masculine singular noun: **buen salario**, **buen trabajo**, **buen hombre**.
**Salario** comes from Latin **salarium** — the allowance given to Roman soldiers to buy salt (**sal**).

**Jubilarse** is a reflexive verb — the reflexive pronoun changes with each subject: **me jubilo, te jubilas, se jubila, nos jubilamos...**
**Se jubiló** = she retired — preterite (past) tense, with the accent on the final syllable marking the preterite.
**El año pasado** = last year — **pasado** (past/last) follows the noun: **la semana pasada**, **el mes pasado**.
From **júbilo** (joy) — compare English "jubilee".
cards.phrase: Ella se jubiló el año pasado.

cards.grammarNote:
**Tener** = to have (an irregular -er verb): **tengo, tienes, tiene, tenemos, tenéis, tienen**.
Note the irregular yo form: **tengo** (not *teno*).
**Buen** shortens before masculine singular nouns: **buen trabajo**, **buen salario** (but **buena idea** with feminine nouns).
**Tener** vs. **haber**: **tener** = to have/possess; **hay** (from haber) = there is/there are.

**Estar desempleado** — use **estar** (not **ser**) for states that can change: unemployment is temporary.
Compare: **Soy médico** (I am a doctor — identity) vs. **Estoy desempleado** (I am unemployed — current state).
**Desempleado** = unemployed — from **des-** (un-) + **empleado** (employed, from **emplear**, to employ).
Alternatives: **estar en paro** (Spain, colloquial), **estar sin trabajo** (to be without work).

**Estudiar** = to study (a regular -ar verb): **estudio, estudias, estudia, estudiamos, estudiáis, estudian**.
**Todos los días** = every day — **todos** (all, masculine plural) agrees with **días** (days).
Compare **estudiar** (to study a subject) vs. **aprender** (to learn/acquire knowledge): **Estudio medicina** (I study medicine) vs. **Aprendo español** (I'm learning Spanish).

**Aprender** = to learn (a regular -er verb): **aprendo, aprendes, aprende, aprendemos, aprendéis, aprenden**.
**Estoy aprendiendo** = I am learning — present progressive: **estar** + gerund (**aprendiendo**).
In Spanish, the simple present often replaces the progressive for ongoing situations: **aprendo español** is equally natural.
Related: **el aprendizaje** (learning/apprenticeship), **aprender de memoria** (to memorize/learn by heart).

**Practicar** = to practice (a sport, hobby, or skill) — use this for non-game activities.
Don't use **jugar** for non-competitive activities: say **practico yoga**, **practico natación**, **practico senderismo**.
For sports framed as competition or play, **jugar** is also correct: **juego al tenis** (I play tennis) or **practico el tenis**.
**Practico** (I practice) — note the spelling change: **c → qu** before **e/i**: **practiqué** (I practiced, preterite).

**Enseñar** = to teach / to show (a regular -ar verb).
Note that **enseñar** means both "to teach" and "to show": **Me enseña la foto** (She shows me the photo) vs. **Enseña español** (She teaches Spanish).
The person who teaches: **el profesor / la profesora** (teacher/professor) — gender forms differ.
Related: **la enseñanza** (teaching/education), **el colegio** (school), **dar clases** (to give classes/teach).

**Construir** belongs to a group of -uir verbs that insert a **y** before vowel endings: **construyo, construyes, construye, construimos, construís, construyen**.
The gerund: **construyendo** (building) — the **y** appears there too.
**Están construyendo** = they are building — present progressive.
Related: **la construcción** (construction), **el constructor / la constructora** (builder), **el edificio** (building).
cards.englishPhrase: They are building a new school.

cards.grammarNote:
**Arreglar** = to fix / to arrange / to tidy (a regular -ar verb).
Context determines the meaning: **arreglar el coche** (to fix the car), **arreglar la habitación** (to tidy the room), **arreglarse** (to get ready/dressed up — reflexive).
**¿Puedes arreglar esto?** — **poder** (can/to be able to) is a stem-changing verb (o→ue): **puedo, puedes, puede, podemos, podéis, pueden**.
**Esto** = this (neuter demonstrative, used when the noun is unspecified).

**Hacer** = to make / to do — one of the most versatile and irregular verbs in Spanish.
Irregular yo form: **hago** (not *haco*).
Full present: **hago, haces, hace, hacemos, hacéis, hacen**.
**Hacer** is used for many activities: **hacer ejercicio** (to exercise), **hacer yoga**, **hacer senderismo**, **hacer la tarea** (to do homework), **hacer una llamada** (to make a call).
**Hago café** — note no article needed: **hacer café**, **hacer ejercicio** (activity as a mass/abstract noun).

**Diseñar** = to design (a regular -ar verb).
The person who designs: **el diseñador / la diseñadora** (designer).
**Páginas web** = websites — literally "web pages". Also: **sitios web** (websites) or just **webs** (colloquial).
Related fields: **el diseño gráfico** (graphic design), **el diseño web** (web design), **el diseño industrial** (industrial design).
Compare with **dibujar** (to draw): **diseñar** implies intentional planning/creation for a purpose.

**Vender** = to sell (a regular -er verb): **vendo, vendes, vende, vendemos, vendéis, venden**.
The person who sells: **el vendedor / la vendedora** (salesperson/seller).
Related: **la venta** (sale), **en venta** (for sale), **comprar** (to buy — the opposite).
Note: **coches** (cars) is the Spain variant; in Latin America you'll hear **carros** or **autos** instead.

**Comprar** = to buy (a regular -ar verb): **compro, compras, compra, compramos, compráis, compran**.
**Necesito comprar** — **necesitar + infinitive** = to need to do something: **necesito estudiar** (I need to study).
**Computadora** = computer (Latin America); in Spain: **el ordenador**. Both are correct.
Related: **la tienda** (store), **el precio** (price), **la oferta** (sale/offer), **las compras** (shopping).
cards.englishPhrase: I need to buy a new computer.

cards.grammarNote:
**Ayudar** = to help (a regular -ar verb).
**¿Puedo ayudarte?** — **ayudarte** is an infinitive with a clitic pronoun attached: **ayudar** + **te** (you).
This is called an "attached pronoun" on an infinitive. Alternatively: **¿Te puedo ayudar?** (same meaning, pronoun before the conjugated verb).
**Puedo** = I can — from **poder** (o→ue stem-changer): **puedo, puedes, puede...**
**Ayudar a** + person: **Te ayudo a encontrarlo** (I'll help you find it).

**Dirigir** = to manage / to direct / to lead (an -ir verb).
Irregular yo form: **dirijo** (not *dirigo*) — the **g** changes to **j** before **o** to preserve the soft sound.
Full present: **dirijo, diriges, dirige, dirigimos, dirigís, dirigen**.
Related: **el director / la directora** (director/manager), **la dirección** (management/direction/address).
Alternatives for "to manage": **gestionar** (to manage/administer), **administrar** (to administer).

**Reunirse** = to meet/gather (a reflexive verb).
Reflexive pronouns: **me reúno, te reúnes, se reúne, nos reunimos, os reunís, se reúnen**.
Note the accent: **reúno, reúnes, reúne, reúnen** — the stress falls on the **ú** in most forms (but NOT in nosotros/vosotros).
**Nos reunimos** = we meet — note no accent here (stress on **ni** is regular).
**Cada lunes** = every Monday — **cada** (each/every) + singular: **cada día**, **cada semana**, **cada mes**.

**Empezar** is a stem-changing verb (e→ie): **empiezo, empiezas, empieza, empezamos, empezáis, empiezan**.
Note: no stem change in **nosotros/vosotros** forms.
**Empezar a + infinitive** = to start doing something: **empiezo a trabajar** (I start working).
Note also the spelling change: **z → c** before **e** in preterite: **empecé** (I started).
Synonym: **comenzar** (identical conjugation pattern — also e→ie).
cards.phrase: Empiezo a trabajar a las ocho.

cards.grammarNote:
**Terminar** = to finish / to end (a regular -ar verb).
**Terminar de + infinitive** = to finish doing something: **termino de trabajar** (I finish working), **terminé de comer** (I finished eating).
Compare: **terminar** (to finish) vs. **empezar** (to start) — natural opposites.
**A las cinco** = at five (o'clock) — telling time: **a la una** (at one), **a las dos/tres...** (at two/three...).
cards.phrase: Termino de trabajar a las cinco.

cards.grammarNote:
**gustar** + infinitive = to like doing something
**Me gusta leer** (I like reading) · **A ella le gusta cantar** (She likes singing)
For clarity with third-person subjects, add **a** + person: **A mi padre le gusta pescar**
**gustar** agrees with what is liked, not the person doing the liking
cards.englishPhrase: I like reading. She likes singing.
cards.phrase: Me gusta leer. A ella le gusta cantar.

cards.grammarNote:
**Relajarse** is a reflexive verb: **me relajo, te relajas, se relaja, nos relajamos...**
**Me relajo** = I relax — the reflexive **me** shows the action reflects back on the subject.
Reflexive verbs for mental/physical states are very common in Spanish: **relajarse** (to relax), **distraerse** (to get distracted), **concentrarse** (to focus), **aburrirse** (to get bored).
**Los fines de semana** = on weekends — **los** + time noun expresses recurrence.
cards.phrase: Me relajo los fines de semana.

cards.grammarNote:
**Pasar tiempo** = to spend time — **pasar** here means "to spend" (time), not "to pass" (by).
**Paso mucho tiempo leyendo** — note the gerund **leyendo** (reading): the activity follows **tiempo** as a gerund, not an infinitive.
**Leyendo** is the gerund of **leer** — the unstressed **i** between vowels becomes **y**: **leer → leyendo**.
Alternative: **dedicar tiempo a + infinitive**: **dedico tiempo a leer** (I dedicate time to reading).
cards.englishPhrase: I spend a lot of time reading.

cards.grammarNote:
**Creativo** is a quality adjective that changes for gender and number: **creativo** (m.sg.) / **creativa** (f.sg.) / **creativos** (m.pl.) / **creativas** (f.pl.).
Note the example: **Ella es muy creativa** — the adjective agrees with **ella** (feminine), so **creativa**.
Use **ser** for permanent character traits: **es creativa** (she is creative by nature).
Related: **la creatividad** (creativity), **crear** (to create), **la creación** (creation).

**Interesante** is an invariable adjective — it does NOT change for gender: **un trabajo interesante** / **una idea interesante** (same form).
Adjectives ending in **-e** or consonants are typically invariable for gender (they still change for number: **interesantes**).
Use **ser** for inherent qualities: **Mi trabajo es interesante** (permanent characteristic).
Related: **interesar** (to interest) — works like **gustar**: **Me interesa la música** (Music interests me).

**Estresante** is another invariable adjective (ending in **-ante**): **un trabajo estresante** / **una situación estresante** — same form for masculine and feminine.
Borrowed from English "stress" → **estrés** (noun, masculine: **el estrés**) → **estresante** (adjective) → **estresarse** (reflexive verb: to get stressed).
Use **ser** for a job's inherent quality: **Mi trabajo es estresante**.
Contrast with **estar estresado/a** (to feel stressed right now — a temporary state).

**Aburrido** changes for gender and number: **aburrido** (m.sg.) / **aburrida** (f.sg.) / **aburridos / aburridas** (plural).
Note: **La reunión fue aburrida** — the adjective agrees with **la reunión** (feminine).
**Fue** is the preterite of both **ser** and **ir** — they share the same preterite forms: **fui, fuiste, fue, fuimos, fuisteis, fueron**. Context clarifies meaning.
**Ser vs. estar with aburrido**: **ser aburrido** = to be a boring (person/thing by nature); **estar aburrido** = to feel bored (current state).

**El salario** = salary — from Latin **salarium** (allowance to buy salt, **sal**), hence the English word "salary".
Also common: **el sueldo** (wage/pay — more colloquial than **salario**).
**¿Cuál es el salario?** — use **cuál** (which/what) before **es** when asking about identity/value (not **qué**).
**Cuál vs. qué**: **¿Cuál es tu nombre?** (What is your name? — selection from a set) vs. **¿Qué es el amor?** (What is love? — definition).

**El horario** = schedule/timetable — from **hora** (hour) + **-ario** (a suffix for things related to something).
**Flexible** is an invariable adjective: **mi horario es flexible** / **mis horarios son flexibles** — same form for masculine/feminine.
**Mi horario es flexible** uses **ser** because schedule type is an inherent characteristic.
Related: **el turno** (shift/turn), **jornada completa** (full-time), **media jornada** (part-time), **hora punta** (rush hour).

**Vacaciones** is almost always used in the plural in Spanish — even for a single trip: **voy de vacaciones** (not *voy de vacación*).
**Ir de vacaciones** = to go on vacation — note **de**, not *en*: **voy de vacaciones** (I'm going on vacation).
**La semana próxima** = next week — **próximo** (next) follows the noun for time: **el mes próximo**, **el año próximo**. Alternatively, **próxima semana** or **la semana que viene** (the week that comes) are also common.
cards.englishPhrase: I'm going on vacation next week.
cards.phrase: Voy de vacaciones la semana próxima.

cards.grammarNote:
**El descanso** = break/rest — from **descansar** (to rest).
**Tomar un descanso** = to take a break — **tomar** (literally "to take/drink") is used in many fixed expressions: **tomar un café** (to have a coffee), **tomar asiento** (to take a seat), **tomar una decisión** (to make a decision).
**Tomemos** is the nosotros imperative (let's take): formed from the subjunctive **tomemos**.
Related: **descansar** (to rest), **el recreo** (school recess/break), **la pausa** (pause).

**La oficina** = office (the room/workplace).
From Latin **officina** (workshop, place of work) — same root as English "office".
**Trabajo en una oficina** — **en** for location (in/at): **en casa** (at home), **en el hospital** (at the hospital).
Related: **el despacho** (personal office/study — Spain), **la empresa** (company/firm), **el edificio de oficinas** (office building).

**Al aire libre** = outdoors / in the open air — a fixed expression: **al** (a + el) + **aire** (air) + **libre** (free/open).
**Al** is the mandatory contraction of **a + el**: **al parque**, **al trabajo**, **al aire libre**.
**Trabajar al aire libre** — many professions involve outdoor work: **jardinero, agricultor, guía turístico, bombero**.
Related: **afuera** (outside), **en el exterior** (on the outside/outdoors — more formal).

**La entrevista** = interview — from **entre** (between) + **vista** (view/sight): a "between-views" / face-to-face meeting.
**Entrevista de trabajo** = job interview — **de trabajo** specifies the type.
**Tengo una entrevista** — **tener** (to have) is used for appointments and scheduled events: **tengo una cita** (I have an appointment), **tengo una reunión** (I have a meeting).
Related: **el entrevistador / la entrevistadora** (interviewer), **el entrevistado / la entrevistada** (interviewee).
cards.englishPhrase: I have a job interview tomorrow.
cards.phrase: Tengo una entrevista de trabajo mañana.

cards.grammarNote:
**El compañero / la compañera** = companion/colleague — from Latin **companio** (one who shares bread: **com-** together + **panis** bread).
**Compañero de trabajo** = work colleague — **de** specifies the context: **compañero de clase** (classmate), **compañero de piso** (roommate).
**Son simpáticos** uses **ser** for personality: **simpático/a** = friendly/pleasant (a permanent trait).
Note the plural adjective agreement: **compañeros** (m.pl.) → **simpáticos** (m.pl.).
cards.phrase: Mis compañeros de trabajo son simpáticos.

cards.grammarNote:
**El cliente / la cliente** — shared-gender noun (the article changes, the word stays the same).
Also acceptable: **la clienta** (feminine form, less common but used in Spain).
**El cliente está contento** — **estar** for current emotional state (not **ser**): **está contento** = is happy right now.
Compare: **ser contento** is not used; for happiness as a state, always use **estar**.
Related: **la clientela** (clientele/customer base), **atender a un cliente** (to serve a customer).

**El equipo** = team (work context) or equipment (sports/technical context) — context clarifies.
**Trabajo en un equipo genial** — **en** for membership: **trabajar en un equipo** (to work on a team), **jugar en un equipo** (to play on a team).
**Genial** = great/awesome (colloquial) — literally "genial/genius-like". An invariable adjective: **un equipo genial** / **una idea genial**.
Note: in team sports, Spanish treats the team as a collective singular: **el equipo juega** (the team plays).
cards.englishPhrase: I work in a great team.

cards.grammarNote:
**El proyecto** = project — from Latin **projectum** (thrown forward): **pro-** (forward) + **jacere** (to throw). Same root as English "project".
**El proyecto está casi terminado** — **estar** + past participle describes a resulting state: **está terminado** (is finished/completed), **está abierto** (is open), **está cerrado** (is closed).
**Terminado** is the past participle of **terminar**, used here as an adjective agreeing with **proyecto** (masculine).
**Casi** = almost/nearly — an adverb, doesn't change form.

**La reunión** = meeting — from **reunir** (to gather/unite): **re-** (again) + **unir** (to unite).
**Tenemos una reunión a las diez** — **tener** is used for scheduled events.
**A las diez** = at ten (o'clock) — plural **las** because it's more than one: **a la una** (at one), **a las dos** (at two), **a las diez** (at ten).
Related: **reunirse** (reflexive: to meet/gather), **convocar una reunión** (to call a meeting).
cards.englishPhrase: We have a meeting at ten.
cards.phrase: Tenemos una reunión a las diez.

cards.grammarNote:
**El tiempo libre** = free time — **libre** (free/unoccupied) as opposed to **ocupado** (busy).
**¿Qué haces en tu tiempo libre?** — **haces** is the tú form of **hacer**: **hago, haces, hace, hacemos...**
Note: **¿Qué haces?** (What do you do?) vs. **¿Qué estás haciendo?** (What are you doing right now?).
**Tiempo** has two meanings: **time** (el tiempo libre) and **weather** (¿Qué tiempo hace? — What's the weather like?). Context always clarifies.
cards.englishPhrase: What do you do in your free time?
